title: Carved sycamore ladle
subtitle: A long-handled cooking ladle in green sycamore
excerpt: A carved green-sycamore ladle with a deep oval bowl and a long straight handle. Axe to sloyd knife to hook knife. Broader than an eating spoon and requires a hook-knife-large for the bowl; otherwise the same progression as a spoon.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the axe-to-knife blank progression. The ladle form is documented in Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and in the Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on domestic treen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
A leather strap charged with abrasive compound, used to maintain a sharp edge between sharpenings.

projectSchedule.title: Carve the ladle
projectSchedule.body: Shape the blank, hollow the bowl, refine the handle.

projectSchedule.title: Oil and cure
projectSchedule.body: Apply raw linseed oil today. The ladle needs two weeks to cure before it is food-safe.

content.text: A carved ladle is the same project as an eating spoon, scaled up. The bowl is deeper and wider, the handle is longer, and the
blank
starts as a bigger piece of
green wood

. Sycamore is the best choice: it carves cleanly when green, dries food-safe pale, and has no strong grain pattern to fight. A 30 cm long billet, 7 cm across at the widest, gives a ladle that holds roughly 60 ml.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Start with a straight-grained sycamore billet 30 cm long and at least 7 cm in diameter. Split or saw it so you have a flat face on one side. The flat face becomes the back of the ladle. The back of the bowl should come from the pith-side of the billet; the front of the bowl (the hollow side) faces outward.
If sycamore is unavailable, birch or ash both work. Birch is slightly softer and easier to hollow. Ash is harder and the grain is coarser, but it dries food-safe and takes oil well.
Roughing the outer shape

Draw the ladle outline on the flat face: an oval bowl roughly 10 cm long and 6 cm wide, and a handle 20 cm long tapering from 25 mm at the neck to 15 mm at the tip. Use push cuts with the carving axe to remove wood from both sides of the handle, leaving it proud of the bowl.

Chop down to the bowl boundary on each side of the neck with stop cuts first. This sets the boundary and prevents the axe from travelling into the bowl section when you take long shaping cuts down the handle.

Turn to the sloyd knife for the fine outer shape. Push cuts along the handle take the section from a rough rectangle to a rounded oval. The back of the bowl is convex; shape it now with push cuts, working from the widest point of the bowl toward the rim on all sides.
Hollowing the bowl

Hold the blank bowl-side up. The large hook knife works in arcs from the centre of the bowl outward toward the rim. Short pull strokes. The knife travels away from your body on each stroke; the blade arc does not approach the hand holding the blank.

Take even depth from the whole bowl rather than driving deep into the centre first. An even bowl is easier to finish than a deep central pit surrounded by thick walls. The finished bowl depth for a cooking ladle is roughly 25 mm.
Use the
strop
every fifteen minutes. A sharp hook knife lifts clean curls. A dull one compresses the wood and leaves a fibrous surface.
Finishing the handle and rim

Refine the handle with push cuts until the cross-section is a comfortable oval in the hand. Round the rim of the bowl so there are no sharp edges. A series of slicing cuts at 45 degrees to the rim face chamfers the edge cleanly.
Drying
Leave the ladle in a paper bag in a cool room for two to three weeks. Do not dry it near a radiator or in sun; rapid drying causes checks (hairline cracks) along the grain.
Finishing
Once dry, sand lightly with 400 grit and apply raw linseed oil inside and out. Wipe off the surplus after one hour. The oil needs two full weeks to cure before the ladle is food-safe.
Variations
Soup ladle: deepen the bowl to 35 mm and widen to 8 cm. Start with a billet 8 cm in diameter.
Birch alternative: same method, slightly softer. Birch dries faster and may need more careful slow-drying management.
Left-handed version: the bowl hook is symmetrical, so the ladle is not handed. Only the handle grip profile needs adjusting if the maker shapes a thumb rest into it.
Care
Wash by hand in warm soapy water and towel-dry. Never soak and never put in the dishwasher. Re-oil with raw linseed oil twice a year or when the wood looks dry.

rows.symptom: Bowl walls are thick on one side and thin on the other
rows.cause: Hook knife working unevenly around the bowl perimeter
rows.fix: Check the rim thickness with your fingertips as you work. Take more from the thick side. Mark the thin side with a pencil so you remember to leave it alone.

rows.symptom: Hook knife cuts in some arcs but not others
rows.cause: Cutting against the grain in certain quadrants of the bowl
rows.fix: Rotate the blank and approach from the opposite direction for the quadrant that is fighting. In a round bowl, two opposite quadrants cut with the grain and two cut against it.

rows.cause: Uneven wall thickness or rapid drying
rows.fix: For small checks, fill with wood glue and clamp. For large cracks down the handle, the piece is decorative only; structural cracking in a ladle handle means it should not be used for heavy pots.
